en.extract:
The Moluccan scops owl (Otus magicus) is an owl found in the Maluku and Lesser Sunda Islands of Indonesia. It closely resembles the Rinjani scops owl.
The following subspecies are recognised:

O. m. kalidupae (Hartert, EJO, 1903) - Kaledupa
O. m. morotensis (Sharpe, 1875) - Morotai
O. m. leucospilus (Gray, GR, 1861) - Halmahera, Ternate, Kasiruta, and Bacan
O. m. obira Jany, 1955 - Obi
O. m. bouruensis (Sharpe, 1875) - Buru
O. m. magicus (Müller, S, 1841) - Seram and Ambon
O. m. albiventris (Sharpe, 1875) - west-central Lesser Sundas
The Wetar scops owl (O. tempestatis) was previously considered a subspecies, but has since been split.
It has spiritual significance to the Pesaguan Dayak people. It is believed to be a sign of misfortune, as its sound is said to be reminiscent of death. This belief may protect the species from being hunted.


References

BirdLife Species Factsheet - Moluccan Scops-owl

The '''Moluccan scops owl''' ('''''Otus magicus''''') is an owl found in the [[Maluku Islands|Maluku]] and [[Lesser Sunda Islands]] of [[Indonesia]]. It closely resembles the [[Rinjani scops owl]].

The following [[subspecies]] are recognised:<ref>{{Cite web |title=Owls – IOC World Bird List |url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/new/bow/owls/ |access-date=2023-01-28 |website=www.worldbirdnames.org}}</ref>

* ''O. m. kalidupae'' <small>([[Ernst Hartert|Hartert, EJO]], 1903)</small> - [[Wakatobi Regency#Kaledupa Island|Kaledupa]]
* ''O. m. morotensis'' <small>([[Richard Bowdler Sharpe|Sharpe]], 1875)</small> - [[Morotai]]
* ''O. m. leucospilus'' <small>([[George Robert Gray|Gray, GR]], 1861)</small> - [[Halmahera]], [[Ternate]], [[Kasiruta]], and [[Bacan]]
* ''O. m. obira'' <small>Jany, 1955</small> - [[Obi (island)|Obi]]
* ''O. m. bouruensis'' <small>(Sharpe, 1875)</small> - [[Buru]]
* ''O. m. magicus'' <small>([[Salomon Müller|Müller, S]], 1841)</small> - [[Seram]] and [[Ambon Island|Ambon]]
* ''O. m. albiventris'' <small>(Sharpe, 1875)</small> - west-central [[Lesser Sundas]]

The [[Wetar scops owl]] (''O. tempestatis'') was previously considered a subspecies, but has since been split.<ref>{{Cite web|title=IOC World Bird List 12.1|website=IOC World Bird List Datasets|language=en-US|doi=10.14344/ioc.ml.12.1}}</ref>

It has spiritual significance to the Pesaguan Dayak people. It is believed to be a sign of misfortune, as its sound is said to be reminiscent of death. This belief may protect the species from being hunted.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Erwinsyah |first=Erwinsyah |last2=Due |first2=Rufina |date=2022-09-06 |title=Ethnobiology of the Pesaguan Dayak Tribe as a Science and Biology Learning Resource |journal=Aquademia |volume=6 |issue=2 |pages=ep22010 |doi=10.30935/aquademia/12427 |issn=2542-4874|doi-access=free }}</ref>
